intro.kicker: Topic 9 of 22 • Chapter: Newton’s Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Newton’s Third Law states that to every action there is always an equal and opposite reaction. The action force Fₐₙ exerted on body A by body B, and the reaction force Fₙₐ exerted on B by A, are equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and simultaneous. Critically, they act on DIFFERENT bodies — they can never cancel each other. This distinction is the single most tested conceptual trap in the entire Newton’s Laws chapter in NEET.

expectedQuestions.description: At least one conceptual MCQ per paper on identifying the correct action-reaction pair or explaining why N and mg on a book are NOT an action-reaction pair. Occasionally a numerical on gun recoil or swimmer force.

timeRequired.description: 30 min theory + examples, 60 min conceptual MCQ practice. The concepts are simple but the application traps require deliberate practice with varied question types.

difficulty.description: Statement is trivial; application is tricky. The difficulty arises in correctly identifying which body each force of a pair acts on, especially in composite systems with normal forces, tensions, and gravity.

nriUsaGap.description: Newton’s Third Law is universal. US AP Physics covers the same content but may frame questions differently. The NEET angle focuses on identifying action-reaction pairs conceptually, which AP also emphasises.

weightage.sectionTitle: Newton’s Third Law — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Appears in every NEET paper. Never skip this topic.
Most common question: identify the correct action-reaction pair.
Second most common: explain why N and mg on a table cannot cancel.

strategySteps.title: Always Draw Two FBDs
strategySteps.description: For every action-reaction question, draw a separate Free Body Diagram for each body. Mark the action force on one body and the corresponding reaction on the other. This mechanically prevents the classic error of thinking N and mg cancel.

strategySteps.title: Three Tests for Action-Reaction Pairs
strategySteps.description: An action-reaction pair must: (1) have equal magnitudes, (2) have opposite directions, (3) act on DIFFERENT bodies. If forces act on the same body, they are NOT an action-reaction pair regardless of their magnitudes.

strategySteps.title: Learn the Classic N vs mg trap
strategySteps.description: N (normal reaction of table on book) and mg (weight of book due to Earth) both act on the book — same body → NOT action-reaction. The correct pairs are: (mg, pull of book on Earth) and (N, push of book on table).

cards.title: Newton’s Third Law Concept Notes
cards.description: Complete notes covering law statement, action-reaction pair identification rules, gun-bullet / swimming / walking examples, and 10 NEET-style MCQs with solutions.

cards.title: NEET Trap: N vs mg
cards.description: One-page focused note on the most-failed conceptual MCQ in Newton’s Laws: are the normal force and weight on a table an action-reaction pair? With four worked problem variants.

cards.title: The Law
cards.coreRevision: To every action, there is always an equal (in magnitude) and opposite (in direction) reaction. Fₐₙ = −Fₙₐ
points: Forces always occur in pairs; a single isolated force is impossible
Action and reaction act on DIFFERENT bodies — they never cancel
The two forces are simultaneous; neither is the cause of the other
cards.example: A gun of mass M fires a bullet of mass m at velocity v. Reaction: gun recoils at V = −mv/M. The bullet’s forward force (action on gun) and gun’s backward force (reaction on bullet) are equal in magnitude.

cards.title: Identifying Action-Reaction Pairs
cards.coreRevision: Action and reaction: (1) equal magnitude, (2) opposite direction, (3) act on different bodies, (4) same type of force (both contact or both gravitational).
points: Book on table: (a) Earth pulls book down [gravity] ↔ book pulls Earth up [gravity] — action-reaction pair
Book on table: (b) Table pushes book up [normal] ↔ book pushes table down [normal] — action-reaction pair
N and mg on the book are NOT a pair — both act on the book (different natures, same body)
cards.example: Swimming: swimmer pushes water backward (action). Water pushes swimmer forward (reaction). Both are contact forces; they act on different bodies (water and swimmer).

curriculum.hintText: Points where AP Physics treatment differs from the NEET conceptual emphasis.

gaps.title: NEET Focuses on Pair Identification
gaps.description: US AP Physics uses Newton’s Third Law primarily in calculations (Atwood machine, connected blocks). NEET additionally tests conceptual identification of pairs in statement-based (assertion-reason) MCQs.
bullets: Practice statement-based MCQs specifically: ‘Statement I: action and reaction cancel each other’ — this is FALSE.
Prepare for questions that give a scenario and ask which pair is the correct action-reaction pair from four options.

questions.question: A book is lying on a table. The action-reaction pair for the force of gravity on the book is:

options.text: The normal force the table exerts on the book
The gravitational pull the book exerts on the Earth
The normal force the book exerts on the table
The weight of the table itself

questions.explanation: The action force is the gravitational pull of the Earth on the book (mg downward, acts on the book). The reaction, by Newton’s Third Law, is the gravitational pull of the book on the Earth (equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, acts on the Earth). The normal force pair is a separate action-reaction pair: book pushes table down ↔ table pushes book up.

questions.question: Newton’s third law of motion is Fₐₙ = −Fₙₐ. Which statement about this is INCORRECT?

options.text: The two forces are equal in magnitude
The two forces act on different bodies
The two forces cancel each other making the net force on the system zero
The forces are of the same nature (both contact or both gravitational)

questions.explanation: The statement that action and reaction cancel each other is incorrect. They act on DIFFERENT bodies; a force can only cancel another force that acts on the SAME body. The internal forces of an action-reaction pair cancel at the SYSTEM level (Newton’s third-law pairs sum to zero in the total system momentum change), but they cannot cancel a force on a single body.

questions.question: A horse pulls a cart with force F. The cart pulls the horse with an equal and opposite force. Then why does the system move?
options: The horse’s force is slightly larger than the cart’s force
The ground exerts a forward friction on the horse’s hooves which is not cancelled by the horse-on-cart force
Newton’s third law is not applicable here
questions.answer: The ground exerts a forward friction on the horse’s hooves which is not cancelled by the horse-on-cart force

questions.question: A swimmer pushes water backward with a force of 200 N. The force with which water pushes the swimmer forward is:
options: Less than 200 N due to water’s density
Zero when the swimmer is at rest

questions.question: In a gun-bullet system initially at rest, the gun recoils with velocity V after firing a bullet with velocity v. If M and m are gun and bullet masses, which equation is correct?
Which of the following is NOT an example of Newton’s third law?
options: A rocket ejects gas downward and the gas pushes the rocket upward
A ball bouncing off a wall with the same speed it hit
A book resting on a table
Swimmer pushing water backward to move forward
questions.answer: A ball bouncing off a wall with the same speed it hit

items.title: State Newton’s Third Law precisely
items.description: Include: equal magnitude, opposite direction, different bodies, simultaneous, and same type of force.

items.title: Identify the correct action-reaction pair in a given scenario
items.description: Use the three-test rule: equal, opposite, different bodies. Practice with book-table, horse-cart, gun-bullet, swimmer-water.

items.title: Explain why N and mg on a book are NOT action-reaction
items.description: Both act on the book (same body). N is contact; mg is gravitational. Different bodies required for action-reaction pair.

items.title: Derive gun recoil velocity using Newton’s Third Law / conservation
items.description: System momentum = 0 (initially at rest). After firing: MV + mv = 0. V = −mv/M. Recoil is opposite to bullet direction.

items.question: If action and reaction are equal, why do they not cancel each other?
items.answer: Forces cancel only if they act on the SAME body. Action and reaction act on DIFFERENT bodies, so they cannot cancel each other. To cancel a force, you need an equal and opposite force on the same body, not on another body.

items.question: Is Newton’s Third Law always valid?
items.answer: In classical mechanics (speeds much less than c and non-quantum systems), the third law holds universally. At relativistic speeds or in quantum electrodynamics, the instantaneous action-reaction picture breaks down because interactions propagate at finite speed through fields, and momentum is carried by the field itself.

items.question: Does Newton’s Third Law apply to non-contact forces like gravity?
items.answer: Yes. The Earth pulls the Moon with a gravitational force, and the Moon pulls the Earth with an equal and opposite gravitational force. These are a valid action-reaction pair even without physical contact.

items.question: Can we identify which force is the ‘action’ and which is the ‘reaction’?
items.answer: No. Newton’s third law is symmetric; neither force is more fundamental. The labels ‘action’ and ‘reaction’ are arbitrary. You can call either force the action; the other is then the reaction. What matters is that they are simultaneous, equal, opposite, and on different bodies.

grounding_summary: Newton’s Third Law is presented on page 192 with statement: to every action there is an equal and opposite reaction, followed by Fₐₙ = −Fₙₐ on page 193.

The OCR pages list 5 key properties: forces occur in pairs, each agent experiences equal opposite force, action and reaction act on different bodies, action alone cannot produce change, and examples include gun recoil, swimming, and walking.

Page 193 explicitly notes practical examples: book on table exerts force equal to its weight (action), table supports book with equal force (reaction); swimming possible due to third law; when gun is fired bullet moves forward (action), gun recoils backward (reaction).
grounding_excerpt: To every action, there is always an equal (in magnitude) and opposite (in direction) reaction.
Forces in nature always occur in pairs. A single isolated force is not possible.
Action and reaction never act on the same body.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Laws of Motion
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source for Newton’s Third Law with official statement, examples, and NEET-aligned coverage of action-reaction pair identification.

sources.title: Laws of Motion Detailed Explanation — Physics Class 11 NEET
sources.why_used: NEET-specific coverage with exam pattern analysis, common MCQ traps on action-reaction identification, and conceptual questions from previous papers.

review.notes: Grounded fully in pages 192-193. Law statement, mathematical form, examples all verified from OCR text. Research confirmed NEET exam trap patterns.
